why: when should i use an object

1_beginner_explanation.summary: An **Object** is a collection of related data and functionality. Instead of storing a single value (like a string or a number), an object lets you store multiple values under a single variable. Think of it like a dictionary: it holds 'keys' (names) and 'values' (the data). We use objects to group related information together, like all the details of a single User.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: A primitive variable is like a sticky note with a single word written on it. An object is like a filing cabinet. The cabinet has a label on the front ('User'), and inside, there are labeled folders ('Name', 'Age', 'Email'). Instead of carrying around 10 different sticky notes, you just carry the single filing cabinet.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When you use curly braces `{}` (object literal syntax), the JavaScript engine allocates a new block of memory in the Heap to store the properties. The variable you assign the object to actually only stores a memory address (a reference) pointing to that location in the Heap.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Objects are Reference Types. The actual key-value pairs live in the Heap, while the variable on the Call Stack holds a pointer to the Heap.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Grouping disconnected variables into a single cohesive entity.

4_common_confusion.trap: Using an Array when you should use an Object, or vice versa. If order matters (a list of things), use an Array `[]`. If labels matter (a single thing with properties), use an Object `{}`.
4_common_confusion.fix: Ask yourself: Am I making a 'list of users'? Use an Array. Am I describing 'one specific user'? Use an Object.

6_edge_case.quirk: Keys in standard objects are ALWAYS converted to strings (or Symbols) under the hood. If you try to use a number or an object as a key, JavaScript will silently coerce it into a string first.
